title: Mechanistic PDE Networks for Discovery of Governing Equations
text: ICML 2025

title: Mechanistic PDE Networks for Discovery of Governing Equations
text:
Introduction
Partial differential equations (PDEs) are the standard interpretable, analytical language for spatio-temporal dynamics across science (Turing 1952). Yet developing a PDE model is still largely a manual task done by domain experts inspecting experimental data, and the explosion of experimental and simulation data has sharpened the need for machine-learning methods that discover governing equations directly (Brunton et al. 2020). Building such models so that they are flexible, handle complex nonlinear data, account for missing physics, and stay robust to noise and missing data remains an open problem.

Turing, Alan. 1952. “The Chemical Basis of Morphogenesis.” Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological Sciences 237 (641): 37–72.

Brunton, Steven L., Bernd R. Noack, and Petros Koumoutsakos. 2020. “Machine Learning for Fluid Mechanics.” Annual Review of Fluid Mechanics 52 (1): 477–508.

Pervez, Adeel, Francesco Locatello, and Stratis Gavves. 2024. “Mechanistic Neural Networks for Scientific Machine Learning.” Forty-First International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML).

Chen, Jiale, Dingling Yao, Adeel Pervez, Dan Alistarh, and Francesco Locatello. 2025. “Scalable Mechanistic Neural Networks.” International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR).
This work extends the solver-in-network idea of Mechanistic Neural Networks — which embedded a parallel ODE solver in a network for ordinary differential equations (Pervez et al. 2024; Chen et al. 2025) — to the much harder setting of partial differential equations.  We develop NeuRLP-PDE, a solver for parallel, native, differentiable solution of linear PDEs over several spatial dimensions in addition to time, and embed it in a discovery architecture, MechNN-PDE, that learns governing PDEs from data.Scaling is the crux: unlike ODEs, PDE memory requirements grow exponentially with dimension and become excessive even for mid-sized grids.
The synthesis has four stated advantages: derivatives are never taken directly on the data and are computed only internally by the solver; PDEs may contain arbitrary differentiable functions, going beyond generalized-linear forms; numerical solving combined with neural networks confers robustness to noise and missing data; and PDE solving is memory-efficient and batch-parallel on GPU via a sparse multigrid-preconditioned iterative solver.

title: Mechanistic PDE Networks for Discovery of Governing Equations
text:
Mechanistic PDE Networks
A Mechanistic PDE Network has three parts: a mechanistic encoder f_{Enc}, a differentiable solver NeuRLP-PDE specialized for linear PDEs, and an optional decoder f_{Dec}. Given spatio-temporal input u_{\text{data}}(t, x_1, \ldots, x_{n_d}), the encoder produces a set of linear PDEs of predefined size. For a single PDE for a function u(t,x), the general form is

\sum_{m\in\mathcal{M}} c_m(t,x;\, u_{\text{data}})\, u_m \;=\; b(t,x;\, u_{\text{data}}),
\tag{1}
where \mathcal{M} is a multi-index set collecting the partial-derivative indices, the u_m are partial derivatives, and the coefficients c_m, b vary over space and time. The encoder also emits boundary parameters and step sizes; all of these — coefficients, initial/boundary conditions, and step sizes — are differentiable.


The coefficients in Equation 1 are space-time varying, which is what makes these linear PDE representations expressive enough to model complex dynamics. Boundary conditions may be Dirichlet, Neumann, or mixed, and in fact any linear constraint subject to well-posedness.
Although the represented PDEs are linear, the network is not restricted to linear PDEs: nonlinearity enters through nonlinear basis functions applied either to the input data or to learned functions of u. The discovery architecture exploits exactly this.







Figure 1: PDE expression building and the discovery architecture. Spatio-temporal data is transformed by a neural network into \tilde{u} and its derivatives, which are combined through a small tree of elementary functions (here a learnable exponent \epsilon) into a PDE expression that is then handed to the solver. The depicted expression is u_t = \tilde{u}^{\,\epsilon}\,\tilde{u}_x with \epsilon the learnable exponent.




From the original paper (Pervez, Gavves & Locatello, 2025).

Solving linear PDEs by differentiable optimization
NeuRLP-PDE reduces PDE solution on a discretized space-time grid to a relaxed, differentiable constrained optimization (Amos and Kolter 2017). The constraints state that (1) the discretized PDE holds at every grid point, (2) the initial/boundary conditions hold, and (3) the solution is smooth (enforced by per-point Taylor expansions); derivatives are computed by central differences — five-point in practice, with forward/backward differences at the edges. Collecting the variables into a vector z, the constraints take matrix form Az = d and are solved in the least-squares sense via the normal equations A^\top A z = A^\top d. To obtain the dual variables needed for differentiation, the solver uses the saddle-point (KKT) formulation of least squares, which lets gradients flow back to the PDE coefficients through the optimization layer (Amos and Kolter 2017).

Amos, Brandon, and J. Zico Kolter. 2017. “OptNet: Differentiable Optimization as a Layer in Neural Networks.” International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 136–45.
The difficulty is scale. The constraint systems grow rapidly with grid size: the paper notes that a PDE on a 32\times32 grid already has 5{,}120 variables and 9{,}212 constraints (about 377 MB in dense double precision), while a 32\times32\times32 grid ($$220k variables, 420k constraints) would need roughly 780 GB, and 64\times64\times64 around 50{,}000 GB — far beyond GPU memory for dense methods.  The authors therefore solve 1D PDEs with a dense solver (memory stays bounded for small grids) but switch to a sparse iterative solver in two or more spatial dimensions.The systems are extremely sparse — about 99.9\% zeros for a 32\times32 matrix and 99.999\% zeros at 64^3 — which is what makes a sparse iterative approach viable.


An iterative sparse multigrid solver
For larger and higher-dimensional grids, NeuRLP-PDE solves the normal equations with FGMRES, the flexible variant of the Krylov solver GMRES (Saad and Schultz 1986), preconditioned by a multigrid V-cycle (Saad 2003; Briggs et al. 2000). A V-cycle works the residual down a sequence of coarse-to-fine grids, taking a few Gauss-Seidel relaxation steps at each level, solving exactly on the coarsest grid, and prolonging the correction back up. The implementation uses Gauss-Seidel relaxation, linear interpolation for restriction and prolongation, power-of-two grid sizes halved per dimension down to a coarsest size of 8 per dimension, and applies the preconditioned FGMRES to the normal-equation matrix A^\top A. General-purpose preconditioners such as incomplete LU/Cholesky are not used because of their memory cost and lack of GPU implementations. The result is an entirely sparse, batch-parallel GPU solver suitable for higher-dimensional PDEs.

Saad, Youcef, and Martin H. Schultz. 1986. “GMRES: A Generalized Minimal Residual Algorithm for Solving Nonsymmetric Linear Systems.” SIAM Journal on Scientific and Statistical Computing 7 (3): 856–69.

Saad, Yousef. 2003. Iterative Methods for Sparse Linear Systems. SIAM.

Briggs, William L., Van Emden Henson, and Steve F. McCormick. 2000. A Multigrid Tutorial. SIAM.

title: Mechanistic PDE Networks for Discovery of Governing Equations
text:
Discovery
Governing-equation discovery seeks PDEs that describe the data while being simple (built from elementary functions) and concise (as few terms as possible) (Brunton et al. 2016). MechNN-PDE restricts the PDE expressions to small tree structures whose nodes are elementary functions with learnable weights — for example, polynomial basis functions up to a fixed degree — but, unlike methods limited to linear combinations of fixed bases, it can represent richer expressions by composing u, its derivatives, and learnable parameters differentiably.

Brunton, Steven L., Joshua L. Proctor, and J. Nathan Kutz. 2016. “Discovering Governing Equations from Data by Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamical Systems.” Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences 113 (15): 3932–37.
A key design choice is to parameterize the input to the PDE expression with a neural network: the data is transformed as \tilde{u} = \mathrm{NN}(u_{\text{data}}), and the expression is computed on \tilde{u} rather than the raw data. This makes the optimization smoother and more robust to noise, with an added loss term keeping \tilde{u} close to the solution u at convergence. The total objective sums a data-fit loss, the parameterization-consistency loss, and an L_1 sparsity penalty on the basis parameters; a concise PDE is then read off by thresholding small coefficients to zero.

title: Mechanistic PDE Networks for Discovery of Governing Equations
text:
Experiments
The method is demonstrated on a range of PDEs in one and two spatial dimensions (plus time). The governing equations used are listed below.



Table 1: PDEs used to demonstrate discovery, with their general forms (reproduced from the original paper).









PDE
General form




Diffusion
u_t = \nu\, u_{xx}


Viscous Burgers
u_t + u\,u_x = \nu\, u_{xx}


Inviscid Burgers
u_t + u\,u_x = 0


Porous medium
u_t = \nabla^2(u^m)


Ginzburg-Landau
\partial_t A = A + \nabla^2 A - (1+i\beta)\,\lvert A\rvert^2 A


Navier-Stokes
\partial_t U + U\cdot\nabla U + \nabla p = \nu\,\nabla^2 U






Setup and baselines. Noise is added as Gaussian noise whose standard deviation is a ratio \sigma_{NR}\in[0,1] of the data RMS value, reported as a percentage \sigma_{NR}\cdot 100\% (Messenger and Bortz 2021). Two metrics evaluate a discovered equation: the true positive ratio \mathrm{TPR} = \mathrm{TP}/(\mathrm{TP}+\mathrm{FN}+\mathrm{FP}), measuring how many terms are correctly identified (Lagergren et al. 2020), and the maximum relative coefficient error E_\infty(\xi) = \max_{j:\hat{\xi}^*_j\neq 0} |\xi_j - \hat{\xi}^*_j|/|\xi_j| (Messenger and Bortz 2021). The baselines are PDEFIND (Rudy et al. 2017) and WeakSINDy (Reinbold et al. 2020), both via the PySINDy implementations (Silva et al. 2020) and both known to be robust to noise in many settings.

Messenger, Daniel A., and David M. Bortz. 2021. “Weak SINDy for Partial Differential Equations.” Journal of Computational Physics 443: 110525.

Lagergren, John H., John T. Nardini, G. Michael Lavigne, Erica M. Rutter, and Kevin B. Flores. 2020. “Learning Partial Differential Equations for Biological Transport Models from Noisy Spatio-Temporal Data.” Proceedings of the Royal Society A 476 (2234): 20190800.

Rudy, Samuel H., Steven L. Brunton, Joshua L. Proctor, and J. Nathan Kutz. 2017. “Data-Driven Discovery of Partial Differential Equations.” Science Advances 3 (4): e1602614.

Reinbold, Patrick A. K., Daniel R. Gurevich, and Roman O. Grigoriev. 2020. “Using Noisy or Incomplete Data to Discover Models of Spatiotemporal Dynamics.” Physical Review E 101 (1): 010203.

Silva, Brian de, Kathleen Champion, Markus Quade, Jean-Christophe Loiseau, J. Nathan Kutz, and Steven Brunton. 2020. “PySINDy: A Python Package for the Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamical Systems from Data.” Journal of Open Source Software 5: 1–4.
Solver sanity check. On the 2D Laplace equation \nabla^2 u = 0 at 32\times32, 64\times64, and 128\times128 resolutions, the multigrid solver reaches L_2 errors of order 10^{-1}, 5\times10^{-2}, and 10^{-2} respectively against a reference finite-difference implementation — errors that shrink with resolution, confirming accuracy comparable to traditional libraries while parallelizing across GPUs.

 

From the original paper (Pervez, Gavves & Locatello, 2025).

Reaction-diffusion
The 2D Ginzburg-Landau reaction-diffusion system is tested in two settings: an easy single-spiral dataset (spatial resolution 64\times64, 128 time steps of size 0.05, generated by a spectral method with D_1=D_2=0.1, \beta=1), where the baselines also do well; and a harder dataset with much more complex patterns (resolution 256\times256, generated with the Basilisk solver from a random initial condition, D_1=D_2=1, \beta=1.5). Both use the same training setup: ResNet parameterizations of the two scalar fields, third-degree polynomial expressions, learned Dirichlet boundary conditions, an L_1 loss with a sparsity weight of 10^{-4}, and Adam at a fixed learning rate of 10^{-5} with a single training cycle (no thresholding-and-refitting).

 

From the original paper (Pervez, Gavves & Locatello, 2025).
On clean easy data, all methods recover the non-zero terms with TPR =1 and small error. As noise grows, PDEFIND degrades the most, its TPR dropping to roughly 0–0.2 on the easy case while the error grows large; WeakSINDy holds up much better and is broadly comparable to MechNN-PDE on the easy case, with a slight edge in error at 80\% noise. The harder case is where the methods separate: neither PDEFIND nor WeakSINDy recovers any significant non-zero terms even on clean data, whereas MechNN-PDE sustains a high TPR through 40\% noise and only drops to about 0.6 at 80\% noise.


Navier-Stokes
For the incompressible Navier-Stokes example, discovery targets the first velocity component, {U_1}_t + U\cdot\nabla U_1 + p_x = \nu\,\nabla^2 U_1, with the pressure gradient supplied and a small viscosity \nu = 0.001. The viscosity is so small that residual terms of its order can register as false positives, holding the TPR just below 1 even though the viscosity and non-zero terms are accurately recovered.

 

From the original paper (Pervez, Gavves & Locatello, 2025).
WeakSINDy is unable to discover the system; MechNN-PDE recovers the equation, although its TPR stays below 1 at low noise because of the small-coefficient false positives noted above.


Beyond fixed basis functions
A distinctive capability is discovery of PDEs that cannot be written as a linear combination of fixed basis functions. For the porous-medium equation u_t = \nabla^2(u^m), the task is to recover the real exponent m — impossible for fixed polynomial bases. Building the PDE expression with a learnable exponent and a neural-network-parameterized gradient, the method recovers m = 2.64 \pm 0.03 from clean data, close to the true value 2.675.
For the 1D diffusion and Burgers’ equations (degree-4 polynomial coefficients for the u_x and u_{xx} terms), the method discovers the true equation in every noise-free case, including the inviscid Burgers’ equation whose shocks are discontinuous; under noise on viscous Burgers’, MechNN-PDE and WeakSINDy are on par.

title: Mechanistic PDE Networks for Discovery of Governing Equations
text:
Conclusion
Mechanistic PDE Networks pair a specialized, parallel, differentiable multigrid PDE solver with a discovery architecture, learning concise governing PDEs from data while remaining robust to noise and extending the class of equations machine-learning methods can model — including expressions, like the porous-medium exponent, that lie outside fixed basis libraries. The authors flag scalability of grid-based PDE methods as the main limitation (a possible remedy being a mix of grid-based and mesh-free techniques), note that the method sometimes trades accuracy for speed, and leave the identifiability of discovered PDEs — the conditions under which discovery is possible — to future work, suggesting an extension of Yao et al. (2024) as a starting point. Full references appear in the margin and are collected below.



Yao, Dingling, Caroline Muller, and Francesco Locatello. 2024. “Marrying Causal Representation Learning with Dynamical Systems for Science.” Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS).

title: CLAI Research Group
text:
Locatello Group · ISTA



Today’s models describe the world as they found it. We want AI that grasps why — learning causal representations and models from data, so it can reason about interventions, domain shifts, and the structure beneath what it sees.

Browse papers Meet the team →
We learn causal structure from data, so that AI can reason about interventions and distribution shifts rather than only fit the conditions it was trained on. Our work spans:

title: About
text:
The Locatello Group, led by Assistant Professor Francesco Locatello at the Institute of Science and Technology Austria (ISTA), works on causal learning and artificial intelligence.
Modern machine learning is still limited to a superficial description of reality that only holds when the experimental conditions are fixed. It largely ignores interventions in the world, domain shifts, and temporal structure. Our group focuses on learning causal representations and causal models from data to address these issues. Through rigorous theory and scalable algorithms, we enable AI agents to understand cause-and-effect relationships, the effect of interventions, and distribution changes underlying the data they encounter.
Advances in causal learning have promising applications in machine learning and artificial intelligence, including robustness, explainability, and fairness in recognition, reasoning, and planning tasks. Most importantly, their impact extends to discovering new scientific knowledge from massive amounts of data and serving as the interface between people and complex systems for decision-making.
Causal learning emerges by discovering structural knowledge about the data-generating process and meaningful abstractions that allow for well-defined causal relations. Research questions span theoretical identifiability and methodological innovations in causal learning, discovering objects and abstract entities, their factors of variations, and their causal relations. We apply these methods to solve open problems in machine learning and enable new applications in the sciences.

Contact

Institute group page: ista.ac.at/en/research/locatello-group

Mechanistic PDE Networks for Discovery of Governing Equations



AI for Science

Dynamical Systems



We present Mechanistic PDE Networks, a model for discovery of governing partial differential equations from data. The method represents spatio-temporal data as space-time dependent linear partial differential equations in neural network hidden representations, which are then solved and decoded for specific tasks. Solving these PDE representations in a compute- and memory-efficient way is the central challenge: we develop NeuRLP-PDE, a native, GPU-capable, parallel, sparse, and differentiable multigrid solver specialized for linear PDEs that acts as a module inside the network. Leveraging the solver, we propose a discovery architecture that can recover nonlinear PDEs in complex settings while remaining robust to noise. We validate discovery on a range of PDEs, including reaction-diffusion and Navier-Stokes equations.





Feb 25, 2025


Adeel Pervez, Efstratios Gavves, Francesco Locatello


ICML


2025
